Without turning from every evil way, and keeping God's statutes, there can be no true godliness; but this must spring from belief of his testimony, as to wrath against all ungodliness and unrighteousness, and his mercy in Christ Jesus.
17.m:
Though the destruction of the kingdom of the ten tribes was only briefly mentioned before, here it is explained in more detail, and the reasons behind it are given. 

The destruction came from God himself. The Assyrians were just doing what God told them to do, like a tool used to punish people. When someone brings sin into a place, they bring trouble too, and they'll have to deal with the consequences.

There are big problems we see around us, like crimes and wars. But even worse are the hidden sins inside people—bad thoughts, desires, and plans. Some sins get noticed by everyone, but others, like being ungrateful or ignoring God, are even more serious. These lead to things like worshiping idols and rejecting God.

True godliness comes from following God’s rules and believing what he says about punishment for wrongdoers and forgiveness through Jesus. We need to stop doing bad things and keep God’s laws if we want to be truly good.

17.o: The terror of the Almighty will sometimes produce a forced or feigned submission in unconverted men; like those brought from different countries to inhabit Israel. But such will form unworthy thoughts of God, will expect to please him by outward forms, and will vainly try to reconcile his service with the love of the world and the indulgence of their lusts. May that fear of the Lord, which is the beginning of wisdom, possess our hearts, and influence our conduct, that we may be ready for every change. Wordly settlements are uncertain; we know not whither we may be driven before we die, and we must soon leave the world; but the righteous hath chosen that good part which shall not be taken from him.
17.m:
Sometimes, people who aren't truly converted will pretend to submit out of fear. This was like what happened with people brought from other places to live in Israel. 

But these people might think poorly of God. They might believe they can please Him just by following rules. And they might try to serve God while still loving the world and giving in to their desires.

May the right kind of fear of the Lord—fear that leads to wisdom—fill our hearts and guide us. We need to be prepared for any changes that come our way.

Worldly things are unstable. We don’t know where life might take us before we die. Soon enough, we’ll have to leave this world behind. But the righteous person has picked the best choice, one that won’t be taken away.

18.o: Hezekiah was a true son of David. Some others did that which was right, but not like David. Let us not suppose that when times and men are bad, they must needs grow worse and worse; that does not follow: after many bad kings, God raised one up like David himself. The brazen serpent had been carefully preserved, as a memorial of God's goodness to their fathers in the wilderness; but it was idle and wicked to burn incense to it. All helps to devotion, not warranted by the word of God, interrupt the exercise of faith; they always lead to superstition and other dangerous evils. Human nature perverts every thing of this kind. True faith needs not such aids; the word of God, daily thought upon and prayed over, is all the outward help we need.
18.m:
Hezekiah was truly like his ancestor David. Other kings tried to do what was right, but none were as good as David. We shouldn't think that things will just keep getting worse if there have been bad rulers before. After many poor leaders, God brought Hezekiah, who was just like David.

The bronze snake from the desert days was kept safe as a reminder of God’s kindness to their ancestors. But it was wrong to worship it. Anything used for devotion that isn’t based on God’s Word leads people away from true faith. It causes them to be superstitious and do other harmful things. People tend to misuse even helpful reminders. 

True faith doesn’t need extra tools. Reading and praying about God’s Word every day is enough to guide us.

18.o: The descent Sennacherib made upon Judah, was a great calamity to that kingdom, by which God would try the faith of Hezekiah, and chastise the people. The secret dislike, the hypocrisy, and lukewarmness of numbers, require correction; such trials purify the faith and hope of the upright, and bring them to simple dependence on God.
18.m: Sennacherib attacked Judah, causing great trouble for the kingdom. This tested Hezekiah’s faith and punished the people. Many were secretly unfaithful, hypocritical, and lazy, so God used these troubles to correct them. Such tests help true believers rely more purely on God.

18.o: Rabshakeh tries to convince the Jews, that it was to no purpose for them to stand it out. What confidence is this wherein thou trustest? It were well if sinners would submit to the force of this argument, in seeking peace with God. It is, therefore, our wisdom to yield to him, because it is in vain to contend with him: what confidence is that which those trust in who stand out against him? A great deal of art there is in this speech of Rabshakeh; but a great deal of pride, malice, falsehood, and blasphemy. Hezekiah's nobles held their peace. There is a time to keep silence, as well as a time to speak; and there are those to whom to offer any thing religious or rational, is to cast pearls before swine. Their silence made Rabshakeh yet more proud and secure. It is often best to leave such persons to rail and blaspheme; a decided expression of abhorrence is the best testimony against them. The matter must be left to the Lord, who has all hearts in his hands, committing ourselves unto him in humble submission, believing hope, and fervent prayer.
18.m:
Rabshakeh tries to convince the Jews that it’s useless for them to resist. What do you think you’re trusting in? It would be good if sinners would listen to this and try to make peace with God. We should give in to God because it’s pointless to fight against him. What good is it to resist someone stronger than us?

There’s a lot of trickery in Rabshakeh’s words, along with pride, hate, lies, and insults. When Hezekiah’s leaders stayed quiet, Rabshakeh became even prouder and more confident. Sometimes it’s better to stay silent around people like that, since talking about religion or reason just wastes time.

Their silence made Rabshakeh feel even better about himself. Often, it’s best to let such people insult and curse freely. Showing strong dislike is the clearest way to oppose them. But we should leave everything else up to God, who controls everyone’s heart. We commit ourselves to him through humble acceptance, hopeful belief, and earnest prayers.

19.o: Hezekiah discovered deep concern at the dishonour done to God by Rabshakeh's blasphemy. Those who speak from God to us, we should in a particular manner desire to speak to God for us. The great Prophet is the great Intercessor. Those are likely to prevail with God, who lift up their hearts in prayer. Man's extremity is God's opportunity. While his servants can speak nothing but terror to the profane, the proud, and the hypocritical, they have comfortable words for the discouraged believer.
19.m:
Hezekiah was very worried about the way Rabshakeh had insulted God. When people speak on God’s behalf to us, we should especially ask them to pray for us. Jesus, the greatest Prophet, is also our greatest Helper when we pray. We are more likely to succeed with God if we sincerely ask him. 

When things seem worst for us, that’s often when God steps in to help. While God’s messengers may warn sinners harshly, they give comfort to those who believe and feel down.

19.o: Prayer is the never-failing resource of the tempted Christian, whether struggling with outward difficulties or inward foes. At the mercy-seat of his almighty Friend he opens his heart, spreads his case, like Hezekiah, and makes his appeal. When he can discern that the glory of God is engaged on his side, faith gains the victory, and he rejoices that he shall never be moved. The best pleas in prayer are taken from God's honour. (2Ki 19:20-34)
19.m:
Prayer is what helps a Christian who is being tested, whether by problems outside or inside them. They tell their all-powerful friend everything at God’s throne, just like Hezekiah did. 

When they see that God’s honor is on their side, their faith wins, and they feel secure knowing nothing will shake them. The strongest arguments in prayer come from thinking about God’s honor. (2 Kings 19:20-34)

19.o: All Sennacherib's motions were under the Divine cognizance. God himself undertakes to defend the city; and that person, that place, cannot but be safe, which he undertakes to protect. The invasion of the Assyrians probably had prevented the land from being sown that year. The next is supposed to have been the sabbatical year, but the Lord engaged that the produce of the land should be sufficient for their support during those two years. As the performance of this promise was to be after the destruction of Sennacherib's army, it was a sign to Hezekiah's faith, assuring him of that present deliverance, as an earnest of the Lord's future care of the kingdom of Judah. This the Lord would perform, not for their righteousness, but his own glory. May our hearts be as good ground, that his word may strike root therein, and bring forth fruit in our lives.
19.m:
All of Sennacherib’s actions were known by God. God promised to protect the city, and anyone protected by Him is safe.

The attack by the Assyrians likely stopped people from planting crops that year. The next year was probably a Sabbath year when no farming happens, so God said the land would provide enough food for both years. 

This promise showed Hezekiah that God would save them now, just as He would take care of them later. God did this not because the people deserved it, but to show His power. Let us have hearts ready to accept God’s promises, allowing His words to grow in us and lead to good deeds.

19.o: That night which followed the sending of this message to Hezekiah, the main body of their army was slain. See how weak the mightiest men are before Almighty God. Who ever hardened himself against Him and prospered? The king of Assyria's own sons became his murderers. Those whose children are undutiful, ought to consider whether they have not been so to their Father in heaven? This history exhibits a strong proof of the good of firm trust and confidence in God. He will afflict, but not forsake his people. It is well when our troubles drive us to our knees. But does it not reprove our unbelief? How unwilling are we to rest on the declaration of Jehovah! How desirous to know in what way he will save us! How impatient when relief is delayed! But we must wait for the fulfilling of his word. Lord, help our unbelief.
19.m:
That night after the message was sent to Hezekiah, most of the enemy soldiers were killed. We see how even the strongest men are weak compared to God. Nobody who has ever tried to resist God has succeeded. 

The king of Assyria’s own sons ended up killing him. People should think about whether they’ve been disrespectful to God if their children are being undutiful.

This story shows how important it is to trust firmly in God. Even though He may cause trouble, He won’t abandon His people. Troubles can push us to pray more. But don’t we often doubt God?

We’re hesitant to rely solely on His promises. Instead, we want to know the exact ways He’ll save us. We get frustrated when help takes too long. Yet we need to be patient and wait for God’s plans to come true. Help us, Lord, with our doubts.

20.o: Hezekiah was sick unto death, in the same year in which the king of Assyria besieged Jerusalem. A warning to prepare for death was brought to Hezekiah by Isaiah. Prayer is one of the best preparations for death, because by it we fetch in strength and grace from God, to enable us to finish well. He wept sorely: some gather from hence that he was unwilling to die; it is in the nature of man to dread the separation of soul and body. There was also something peculiar in Hezekiah's case; he was now in the midst of his usefulness. Let Hezekiah's prayer, see Isa 38. interpret his tears; in that is nothing which is like his having been under that fear of death, which has bondage or torment. Hezekiah's piety made his sick-bed easy. “O Lord, remember now;” he does not speak as if God needed to be put in mind of any thing by us; nor, as if the reward might be demanded as due; it is Christ's righteousness only that is the purchase of mercy and grace. Hezekiah does not pray, Lord, spare me; but, Lord, remember me; whether I live or die, let me be thine. God always hears the prayers of the broken in heart, and will give health, length of days, and temporal deliverances, as much and as long as is truly good for them. Means were to be used for Hezekiah's recovery; yet, considering to what a height the disease was come, and how suddenly it was checked, the cure was miraculous. It is our duty, when sick, to use such means as are proper to help nature, else we do not trust God, but tempt him. For the confirmation of his faith, the shadow of the sun was carried back, and the light was continued longer than usual, in a miraculous manner. This work of wonder shows the power of God in heaven as well as on earth, the great notice he takes of prayer, and the great favour he bears to his chosen.
20.m:
Hezekiah was very sick and close to dying in the same year that the king of Assyria attacked Jerusalem. Isaiah came to warn Hezekiah about his sickness. Praying is one of the best ways to get ready for death because it helps us ask God for strength and grace to end our lives well. Hezekiah cried hard. Some think he did not want to die. People naturally fear the separation of their souls and bodies. In Hezekiah’s case, there was more to worry about since he was still useful. We can learn about Hezekiah’s fears from his prayers recorded in Isaiah 38. His tears show he had concerns about death. But Hezekiah’s devotion made his illness easier to bear. 

“Lord, please remember me,” he prayed. He didn’t mean God needs reminding or that he deserved anything. Instead, he asked God to keep him in mind, whether Hezekiah lived or died. God listens to those who are humble and broken-hearted. He gives health, long life, and other blessings as He sees fit. To heal Hezekiah, God worked a miracle since the illness was so severe. When we’re sick, we should seek proper medical care. Not doing so would be tempting God. 

To strengthen Hezekiah’s faith, God caused the sun’s shadow to move backward and extend daylight hours. This showed God’s power both in heaven and on Earth. It proved God pays attention to prayers and loves His people dearly.

20.o: The king of Babylon was at this time independent of the king of Assyria, though shortly after subdued by him. Hezekiah showed his treasures and armour, and other proofs of his wealth and power. This was the effect of pride and ostentation, and departing from simple reliance on God. He also seems to have missed the opportunity of speaking to the Chaldeans, about Him who had wrought the miracles which excited their attention, and of pointing out to them the absurdity and evil of idolatry. What is more common than to show our friends our houses and possessions? But if we do this in the pride of ours hearts, to gain applause from men, not giving praise to God, it becomes sin in us, as it did in Hezekiah. We may expect vexation from every object with which we are unduly pleased. Isaiah, who had often been Hezekiah's comforter, is now is reprover. The blessed Spirit is both, Joh 16:7, 8. Ministers must be both, as there is occasion. Hezekiah allowed the justice of the sentence, and God's goodness in the respite. Yet the prospect respecting his family and nation must have given him many painful feelings. Hezekiah was indeed humbled for the pride of his heart. And blessed are the dead who die in the Lord; for they rest from their labours, and their works do follow them.
20.m:
The king of Babylon was independent from the king of Assyria back then, even though he would soon be defeated by him. Hezekiah showed off his riches and weapons, signs of his wealth and strength. This was because of pride and showing off, moving away from trusting only in God. 

He could have told the Chaldeans about the one who performed the miracles that caught their interest, and shown them how silly and wrong worshiping idols is. It’s normal to show our friends around our homes and possessions. But if we do this out of pride, seeking human approval instead of praising God, it turns into sin, just like it did for Hezekiah. When we take too much pleasure in what we have, trouble usually follows.

Isaiah, who used to comfort Hezekiah, now speaks to correct him. The Holy Spirit can do both—comfort and correct—as seen in John 16:7, 8. Ministers need to do both as well, depending on the situation. Hezekiah agreed with the punishment and thanked God for giving him extra time. Still, thinking about his family and people must have made him very sad.

Hezekiah became humble because of his pride. Those who die while following the Lord are truly blessed. They get to rest from all their work, and their good deeds continue to matter.

21.o: Young persons generally desire to become their own masters, and to have early possession of riches and power. But this, for the most part, ruins their future comfort, and causes mischief to others. It is much happier when young persons are sheltered under the care of parents or guardians, till age gives experience and discretion. Though such young persons are less indulged, they will afterwards be thankful. Manasseh wrought much wickedness in the sight of the Lord, as if on purpose to provoke him to anger; he did more evil than the nations whom the Lord destroyed. Manasseh went on from bad to worse, till carried captive to Babylon. The people were ready to comply with his wishes, to obtain his favour and because it suited their depraved inclinations. In the reformation of large bodies, numbers are mere time-servers, and in temptation fall away.
21.m:
Young people often want to be independent and get rich or powerful right away. But this usually ruins their happiness later and hurts others too. It’s better when young people stay under their parents’ or guardians' care until they grow older and wiser. Even though they might feel restricted now, they’ll be grateful later.

Manasseh did many evil things that angered God. He was even worse than the nations God had destroyed. His actions got so bad that he was taken captive to Babylon. People followed what he wanted because they hoped to please him and because it matched their own sinful desires. When big groups try to change, lots of people just go along with whatever seems easiest at the moment and give up easily when faced with trouble.

21.o: Here is the doom of Judah and Jerusalem. The words used represent the city emptied and utterly desolate, yet not destroyed thereby, but cleansed, and to be kept for the future dwelling of the Jews: forsaken, yet not finally, and only as to outward privileges, for individual believers were preserved in that visitation. The Lord will cast off any professing people who dishonour him by their crimes, but never will desert his cause on earth. In the book of Chronicles we read of Manasseh's repentance, and acceptance with God; thus we may learn not to despair of the recovery of the greatest sinners. But let none dare to persist in sin, presuming that they may repent and reform when they please. There are a few instances of the conversion of notorious sinners, that none may despair; and but few, that none may presume.
21.m:
Here is what will happen to Judah and Jerusalem. The words describe the city being empty and completely deserted, but not totally destroyed. It was cleaned out and set aside for the future home of the Jewish people. Forsaken, but not forever, just the outward privileges lost because of their sins. 

The Lord will reject any group of people who dishonor him through their wrongdoings. But he will never abandon his work on Earth. In the book of Chronicles, we see that Manasseh repented and was accepted back by God. This teaches us not to give up hope for even the biggest sinners. However, no one should keep doing evil things, thinking they can always repent later.

There are some examples of very sinful people turning around, which shows that no one should lose hope. But there aren't many such cases, so no one should think they can get away with sinning either.

21.o: Amon profaned God's house with his idols; and God suffered his house to be polluted with his blood. How unrighteous soever they were that did it, God was righteous who suffered it to be done. Now was a happy change from one of the worst, to one of the best of the kings of Judah. Once more Judah was tried with a reformation. Whether the Lord bears long with presumptuous offenders, or speedily cuts them off in their sins, all must perish who persist in refusing to walk in his ways.
21.m:
Amon brought idols into God’s temple, making it unholy. And God let his temple get dirty with Amon’s evil deeds. Even though those who did these things were wrong, God was still right to allow it.

Now there was a good turn for Judah after having one of its worst kings. The nation got another chance at fixing things. It didn’t matter if God waited a while before punishing sinners or dealt with them quickly—anyone who kept disobeying him would face destruction.

22.o: The different event of Josiah's early succession from that of Manasseh, must be ascribed to the distinguishing grace of God; yet probably the persons that trained him up were instruments in producing this difference. His character was most excellent. Had the people joined in the reformation as heartily as he persevered in it, blessed effects would have followed. But they were wicked, and had become fools in idolatry. We do not obtain full knowledge of the state of Judah from the historical records, unless we refer to the writings of the prophets who lived at the time. In repairing the temple, the book of the law was found, and brought to the king. It seems, this book of the law was lost and missing; carelessly mislaid and neglected, as some throw their Bibles into corners, or maliciously concealed by some of the idolaters. God's care of the Bible plainly shows his interest in it. Whether this was the only copy in being or not, the things contained in it were new, both to the king and to the high priest. No summaries, extracts, or collections out of the Bible, can convey and preserve the knowledge of God and his will, like the Bible itself. It was no marvel that the people were so corrupt, when the book of the law was so scarce; they that corrupted them, no doubt, used arts to get that book out of their hands. The abundance of Bibles we possess aggravates our national sins; for what greater contempt of God can we show, than to refuse to read his word when put into our hands, or, reading it, not to believe and obey it? By the holy law is the knowledge of sin, and by the blessed gospel is the knowledge of salvation. When the former is understood in its strictness and excellence, the sinner begins to inquire, What must I do to be saved? And the ministers of the gospel point out to him Jesus Christ, as the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.
22.m:
Josiah became king earlier than Manasseh did because of God’s special favor. Likely, the people who raised him helped shape his good character. 

If everyone had supported Josiah’s reforms as strongly as he did, great results would have happened. Instead, the people remained wicked and foolish about worshiping idols.

To fully understand Judah’s situation, we need to look at what the prophets said during that time too.

While fixing the temple, workers found the book of the law. They gave it to King Josiah. This book seemed to be lost or hidden away, much like how some people ignore their Bibles today. 

God clearly cares about the Bible, showing us He values it very much. Whether this was the only copy around or not, both the king and the high priest learned new things from it. Summaries or parts of the Bible cannot teach us about God as well as the whole Bible can.

It’s no surprise that people were so sinful when the book of the law was hard to find. Those who led people astray likely hid the book. Having many Bibles makes our nation’s sins worse, since refusing to read God’s word or not believing and following it shows great disrespect.

Knowing the law helps us see our sins. Knowing the gospel tells us how to be saved. Once someone understands the law’s demands, they wonder how to be saved. Then, church leaders tell them about Jesus, who fulfills the law for those who trust Him.

22.o: The book of the law is read before the king. Those best honour their Bibles, who study them; daily feed on that bread, and walk by that light. Convictions of sin and wrath should put us upon this inquiry, What shall we do to be saved? Also, what we may expect, and must provide for. Those who are truly apprehensive of the weight of God's wrath, cannot but be very anxious how they may be saved. Huldah let Josiah know what judgments God had in store for Judah and Jerusalem. The generality of the people were hardened, and their hearts unhumbled, but Josiah's heart was tender. This is tenderness of heart, and thus he humbled himself before the Lord. Those who most fear God's wrath, are least likely to feel it. Though Josiah was mortally wounded in battle, yet he died in peace with God, and went to glory. Whatever such persons suffer or witness, they are gathered to the grave in peace, and shall enter into the rest which remaineth for the people of God.
22.m:
The book of the law is read to the king. People honor their Bibles most by studying them. They eat from that bread and follow its light. When we realize our sins and God’s anger, we should ask, “What can we do to be saved?” We need to find out what we can expect and prepare for. True fear of God’s anger makes us eager to learn how we might be saved. 

Huldah told Josiah about the punishments God planned for Judah and Jerusalem. Most people were stubborn and unrepentant, but Josiah was humble and concerned. He cared deeply and showed true remorse. This kind of caring heart leads to humility before God. Those who fear God’s anger most are less likely to experience it.

Even though Josiah was badly hurt in battle, he died at peace with God and entered heaven. Such people may face suffering, but they die in peace and go to God’s eternal rest.

23.o: Josiah had received a message from God, that there was no preventing the ruin of Jerusalem, but that he should only deliver his own soul; yet he does his duty, and leaves the event to God. He engaged the people in the most solemn manner to abolish idolatry, and to serve God in righteousness and true holiness. Though most were formal or hypocritical herein, yet much outward wickedness would be prevented, and they were accountable to God for their own conduct.
23.m:
Josiah got a message from God saying he couldn't stop Jerusalem from being destroyed, but he could save himself. Still, Josiah did what was right and left the outcome up to God. 

He made the people promise to get rid of all the idols and worship God faithfully. Even though many people pretended to follow God, a lot of bad behavior would still be stopped. Everyone was responsible to God for their actions.

23.o: What abundance of wickedness in Judah and Jerusalem! One would not have believed it possible, that in Judah, where God was known, in Israel, where his name was great, in Salem, in Zion, where his dwelling-place was, such abominations should be found. Josiah had reigned eighteen years, and had himself set the people a good example, and kept up religion according to the Divine law; yet, when he came to search for idolatry, the depth and extent were very great. Both common history, and the records of God's word, teach, that all the real godliness or goodness ever found on earth, is derived from the new-creating Spirit of Jesus Christ.
23.m:
There is so much evil in Judah and Jerusalem! It’s hard to believe that in Judah, where God was known, in Israel, where God’s name was respected, and in Salem and Zion, where God lived among them, there could still be such terrible things happening. 

King Josiah ruled for eighteen years. He led by example and followed God’s laws. But even then, when he looked for false worship, he found it widespread and deep-rooted. 

Both history books and the Bible show us that true goodness and godliness come only from the Holy Spirit of Jesus Christ.

23.o: Josiah's zeal extended to the cities of Israel within his reach. He carefully preserved the sepulchre of that man of God, who came from Judah to foretell the throwing down of Jeroboam's altar. When they had cleared the country of the old leaven of idolatry, then they applied themselves to the keeping of the feast. There was not holden such a passover in any of the foregoing reigns. The revival of a long-neglected ordinance, filled them with holy joy; and God recompensed their zeal in destroying idolatry with uncommon tokens of his presence and favour. We have reason to think that during the remainder of Josiah's reign, religion flourished.
23.m:
Josiah's zeal spread to the cities of Israel he could reach. He took care to protect the tomb of the prophet who came from Judah to predict the destruction of Jeroboam’s altar. After clearing the land of old idols, they focused on celebrating the Passover festival. 

No previous king held a Passover like this one. Bringing back an ordinance that had been ignored for so long made people very happy. God rewarded their efforts to get rid of false gods by showing special favor and presence. 

We believe that during the rest of Josiah’s time as king, religion continued to grow strong.

23.o: Upon reading these verses, we must say, Lord, though thy righteousness be as the great mountains, evident, plainly to be seen, and past dispute; yet thy judgments are a great deep, unfathomable, and past finding out. The reforming king is cut off in the midst of his usefulness, in mercy to him, that he might not see the evil coming upon his kingdom: but in wrath to his people, for his death was an inlet to their desolations. (2Ki 23:31-37)
23.m: When we read these verses, we should say, Lord, even though your righteousness is clear and obvious, like towering mountains, your judgments are deep and hard to understand, like the ocean. The good king was taken away before he could finish helping, which was merciful for him because he did not have to witness the trouble coming to his kingdom. But it was sad for his people because his death led to more problems for them. (2Ki 23:31-37)

23.o: After Josiah was laid in his grave, one trouble came on another, till, in twenty-two years, Jerusalem was destroyed. The wicked perished in great numbers, the remnant were purified, and Josiah's reformation had raised up some to join the few who were the precious seed of their future church and nation. A little time, and slender abilities, often suffice to undo the good which pious men have, for a course of years, been labouring to effect. But, blessed be God, the good work which he begins by his regenerating Spirit, cannot be done away, but withstands all changes and temptations.
23.m:
After Josiah died, more troubles came one after another until, in twenty-two years, Jerusalem was destroyed. Many wicked people died, while those left behind became better. Some of Josiah’s reforms helped raise up a few good people who would become part of the future church and nation. 

Sometimes, it doesn’t take long or much effort to undo the good that godly people worked hard to achieve over many years. But thank God, the good work He starts through His renewing Spirit cannot be undone. It stands strong against all changes and temptations.

24.o: If Jehoiakim had served the Lord, he had not been servant to Nebuchadnezzar. If he had been content with his servitude, and true to his word, his condition had been no worse; but, rebelling against Babylon, he plunged himself into more trouble. See what need nations have to lament the sins of their fathers, lest they smart for them. Threatenings will be fulfilled as certainly as promises, if the sinner's repentance prevent not. (2Ki 24:8-20)
24.m:
If Jehoiakim had obeyed the Lord, he would not have been forced to serve Nebuchadnezzar. If he had stayed loyal and kept his promises, things would not have gotten worse. But by rebelling against Babylon, he made his situation much harder. 

Nations should remember the mistakes of their ancestors so they do not suffer because of them. God’s warnings will come true just like His promises, unless the person changes their ways.

24.o: Jehoiachin reigned but three months, yet long enough to show that he justly smarted for his fathers' sins, for he trod in their steps. His uncle was intrusted with the government. This Zedekiah was the last of the kings of Judah. Though the judgments of God upon the three kings before him might have warned him, he did that which was evil, like them. When those intrusted with the counsels of a nation act unwisely, and against their true interest, we ought to notice the displeasure of God in it. It is for the sins of a people that God hides from them the things that belong to the public peace. And in fulfilling the secret purposes of his justice, the Lord needs only leave men to the blindness of their own minds, or to the lusts of their own hearts. The gradual approach of Divine judgments affords sinners space for repentance, and believers leisure to prepare for meeting the calamity, while it shows the obstinacy of those who will not forsake their sins.
24.m:
Jehoiachin ruled for only three months, but that was long enough to show that he deserved punishment for his father’s sins because he followed in their footsteps. 

His uncle took over the government. Zedekiah was the last king of Judah. Even though God punished the three kings before him, Zedekiah still acted wickedly like them. 

When leaders make bad choices that hurt their country, we should see it as God’s warning. People suffer God’s judgment because of their sins. To carry out His plans for justice, God lets people stay blind to what is best for everyone. 

As God brings punishments closer, He gives sinners time to turn away from their wrongdoings. Believers get time to prepare too. But some stubborn people refuse to stop doing evil things.

25.o: Jerusalem was so fortified, that it could not be taken till famine rendered the besieged unable to resist. In the prophecy and Lamentations of Jeremiah, we find more of this event; here it suffices to say, that the impiety and misery of the besieged were very great. At length the city was taken by storm. The king, his family, and his great men escaped in the night, by secret passages. But those deceive themselves who think to escape God's judgments, as much as those who think to brave them. By what befell Zedekiah, two prophecies, which seemed to contradict each other, were both fulfilled. Jeremiah prophesied that Zedekiah should be brought to Babylon, Jer 32:5; 34:3; Ezekiel, that he should not see Babylon, Eze 12:13. He was brought thither, but his eyes being put out, he did not see it.
25.m:
Jerusalem was very well protected, but it fell because hunger made the people inside too weak to fight back anymore. We can learn more about what happened from the book of Jeremiah and the book of Lamentations. Here, we just need to know that the people inside the city were very bad and miserable.

Finally, the city was captured during an attack at night. The king, his family, and important leaders escaped through hidden tunnels. But people who try to avoid God’s punishment will fail, just like those who think they can face it without consequences.

Two different predictions came true for King Zedekiah. One prophet said he would go to Babylon, while another said he wouldn’t see the city. Both were right. Zedekiah was taken to Babylon, but his eyes were gouged out first, so he couldn't actually see the place.

25.o: The city and temple were burnt, and, it is probable, the ark in it. By this, God showed how little he cares for the outward pomp of his worship, when the life and power of religion are neglected. The walls of Jerusalem were thrown down, and the people carried captive to Babylon. The vessels of the temple were carried away. When the things signified were sinned away, what should the signs stand there for? It was righteous with God to deprive those of the benefit of his worship, who had preferred false worships before it; those that would have many altars, now shall have none. As the Lord spared not the angels that sinned, as he doomed the whole race of fallen men to the grave, and all unbelievers to hell, and as he spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, we need not wonder at any miseries he may bring upon guilty nations, churches, or persons.
25.m:
The city and temple were destroyed, and likely the ark inside too. This shows that God doesn't care much about fancy religious ceremonies if the true spirit of religion is ignored. 

Jerusalem’s walls were torn down, and its people were taken captive to Babylon. The temple’s treasures were also stolen. When the real meaning of these symbols was lost through sin, what use were the symbols themselves?

God was right to take away the benefits of his worship from those who chose other gods over him. Those who wanted many altars will now have none. Just as God did not spare sinful angels, condemned all fallen humans to death, and even gave up his own son Jesus for us, we shouldn’t be surprised by any troubles he brings on guilty nations, churches, or individuals.

25.o: The king of Babylon appointed Gedaliah to be the governor and protector of the Jews left their land. But the things of their peace were so hidden from their eyes, that they knew not when they were well off. Ishmael basely slew him and all his friends, and, against the counsel of Jeremiah, the rest went to Egypt. Thus was a full end made of them by their own folly and disobedience; see Jeremiah chap. 40 to 45 Jehoiachin was released out of prison, where he had been kept 37 years. Let none say that they shall never see good again, because they have long seen little but evil: the most miserable know not what turn Providence may yet give to their affairs, nor what comforts they are reserved for, according to the days wherein they have been afflicted. Even in this world the Saviour brings a release from bondage to the distressed sinner who seeks him, bestowing foretastes of the pleasures which are at his right hand for evermore. Sin alone can hurt us; Jesus alone can do good to sinners.
25.m:
The king of Babylon put Gedaliah in charge to protect the Jews who stayed in their land. But the people couldn’t see what was good for them. Ishmael killed Gedaliah and his friends. Against Jeremiah’s advice, the others fled to Egypt. So, through their own foolishness and disobedience, the Jews ended up destroyed. See Jeremiah chapters 40 to 45.

Jehoiachin was freed after being in prison for 37 years. Don’t think you’ll never get better just because bad times have lasted a long time. The worst-off might still find hope, as God can always bring unexpected turns to their lives. In this life, Jesus saves those who seek him, giving them tastes of the joy waiting forever on his right side. Only sin hurts us, only Jesus helps sinners.
